summary: Clinical Architecture supports HTI-5's deregulatory actions to advance interoperability and reduce burden, while emphasizing the need to focus next on data quality and fitness-for-use. They propose incorporating structured, standards-based data quality assessment, such as the USCDI v3 Data Quality Rubric and the HL7-balloted PIQI Framework, into future certification programs.
key_arguments: HTI-5 advances API-based exchange and reduces regulatory burden, reinforcing interoperability.
The next challenge is ensuring exchanged data is trustworthy, complete, and fit for use.
USCDI elements alone do not guarantee data quality or usability for downstream purposes.
Automated, standards-based data quality assessment is feasible, scalable, and actionable.
recommendations: Consider referencing the PIQI Framework as an optional capability in the Certification Program to encourage data quality assessment.
Engage with the USCDI v3 Data Quality Rubric and HL7-balloted PIQI Framework initiatives to advance national data quality expectations.
Incorporate structured data quality assessment thoughtfully in HTI-6 after real-world beta testing and adoption.
notable_quote: The country is ready for interoperability that is not only connected—but trusted.
